titleStmt.title: Letter from T.H. Milroy to the under-secretary, 8 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: T.H. Milroy

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Thomas Hugh Milroy (1869-1950), Professor of Physiology at Queen's University Belfast to Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), Under-Secretary for Ireland. In May 1916 Nathan resigned as Under-Secretary, along with his Chief-Secretary Augustine Birrell, as a consequence of the outbreak of the Easter Rising.Milroy's letter is a response to correspondence arising from his submission of his annual Return of Experiments. In the letter he writes that he had omitted to mention that the animals used in the experiments were killed before recovery from anaesthesia.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/4381

correspAction.placeName: Physiology Department, Queen’s University, Belfast, Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Physiology Dep mt Queen's University Belfast 8 th March 1916 The Under Secretary Dublin Castle
Reply to communication N o 3445 Sir I regret that, through
inadvertence I omitted to
state on my 'Return of
Experiments' that the animals
(rabbits) operated on during
anaesthesia and under Certificate
C were killed before recovery
from the anaesthesia. I have
now stated this on my 'Return'.
I am
Yours faithfully T. H. Milroy
